skills.teks_description: Describe one-dimensional motion using distance, displacement, speed, velocity, and acceleration
skills.prompt: This topic covers kinematics for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.5(C)). Students should describe and analyze motion quantitatively using kinematic equations. Kinematic equations (constant acceleration): v = v0 + at, x = x0 + v0t + ½at², v² = v0² + 2a(x - x0), x = ½(v + v0)t. Key distinctions: distance (scalar, total path length) vs. displacement (vector, change in position); speed (scalar) vs. velocity (vector); average vs. instantaneous quantities. Motion graphs: position-time (slope = velocity, curvature indicates acceleration), velocity-time (slope = acceleration, area under curve = displacement), acceleration-time (area = change in velocity). Multi-dimensional motion: resolve vectors into components, treat each dimension independently. Free fall: a = g = 9.8 m/s² downward. Problem-solving strategy: identify known/unknown quantities, select appropriate kinematic equation, solve algebraically before substituting numbers, check units and reasonableness. Vocabulary: kinematics, displacement, velocity, acceleration, scalar, vector, component, projectile, free fall, reference frame, uniform motion. AP exam focus: qualitative reasoning about motion graphs, ranking tasks, multi-step problems connecting kinematics to dynamics. Common misconceptions: acceleration and velocity always point in the same direction; zero velocity means zero acceleration.

skills.teks_description: Calculate force effects using free body diagrams and Newton's second law
skills.prompt: This topic covers dynamics (forces and Newton's laws) for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.5(F)). Students should analyze forces quantitatively using free body diagrams and Newton's laws. Newton's First Law: objects maintain their state of motion unless a net external force acts (inertia). Newton's Second Law: ΣF = ma (vector equation); net force equals mass times acceleration. Newton's Third Law: forces come in action-reaction pairs (equal magnitude, opposite direction, on different objects). Free body diagram procedure: identify the system, draw all external forces acting ON the object (gravity, normal, friction, tension, applied, spring). Common force types: gravitational (Fg = mg), normal (perpendicular to surface), kinetic friction (fk = μkN), static friction (fs ≤ μsN), tension, spring (F = -kx). Problem-solving: draw FBD, choose coordinate system (often align with acceleration), write ΣFx = max and ΣFy = may, solve system of equations. Inclined planes: decompose gravity into components parallel and perpendicular to surface. Atwood machines and pulley systems: apply Newton's second law to each mass separately. Vocabulary: force, net force, inertia, mass, weight, normal force, friction, tension, equilibrium, free body diagram, coefficient of friction. Common misconceptions: force is needed to maintain motion; Newton's third law pairs cancel out.

skills.teks_description: Describe and analyze uniform circular and projectile motion; Phy.5(H) gravitational force
skills.prompt: This topic covers circular motion and gravitation for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.5(D)). Students should analyze objects moving in circular paths and gravitational interactions. Uniform circular motion: constant speed, changing velocity direction, centripetal acceleration ac = v²/r directed toward center. Centripetal force: Fc = mv²/r is the net force toward the center (provided by tension, gravity, friction, normal force, or a combination). Period and frequency: T = 2πr/v, f = 1/T, v = 2πr/T. Newton's law of universal gravitation: Fg = Gm1m2/r², where G = 6.674 × 10^-11 N·m²/kg². Gravitational field: g = GM/r². Orbital motion: gravitational force provides centripetal force (GMm/r² = mv²/r), leading to v = √(GM/r). Kepler's third law: T² ∝ r³. Key concepts: apparent weightlessness in orbit (free fall), geosynchronous orbits, escape speed, gravitational potential energy U = -GMm/r. Banked curves: component of normal force provides centripetal force. Conical pendulum: tension component provides centripetal force. Vocabulary: centripetal, centrifugal, period, frequency, orbit, satellite, gravitational field, geosynchronous, escape speed, banked curve. Common misconceptions: there is no gravity in space; centrifugal force is real in an inertial frame; satellites are beyond Earth's gravity.

skills.teks_description: Investigate and calculate mechanical, kinetic, and potential energy
skills.prompt: This topic covers energy for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.7(B)). Students should investigate and calculate mechanical, kinetic, and potential energy and apply conservation principles. Forms of mechanical energy: kinetic energy KE = ½mv², gravitational potential energy PE = mgh (near surface) or U = -GMm/r (general), elastic potential energy PE = ½kx². Conservation of mechanical energy: in absence of non-conservative forces, KEi + PEi = KEf + PEf. Work-energy theorem: Wnet = ΔKE. Work: W = Fd cos(θ), area under F vs. d graph. Power: P = W/t = Fv, measured in watts. Energy bar charts: visual representation of energy transformations at different points in a process. Non-conservative forces (friction): convert mechanical energy to thermal energy; total energy is still conserved but mechanical energy decreases. Springs: Hooke's law F = -kx; elastic PE = ½kx². Key concepts: reference point for PE is arbitrary (choose convenient zero), energy is scalar (no direction), identify system boundaries for conservation analysis. Vocabulary: kinetic energy, potential energy, mechanical energy, conservation of energy, work, power, work-energy theorem, dissipation, internal energy, system, conservative force. Common misconceptions: energy is a substance; KE is always conserved in collisions; potential energy belongs to a single object rather than a system.

skills.teks_description: Calculate impulse and momentum in physical systems
skills.prompt: This topic covers momentum for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.7(D)). Students should calculate impulse and momentum and apply conservation of momentum. Linear momentum: p = mv, a vector quantity (has direction). Impulse: J = FΔt = Δp; area under F vs. t graph equals impulse. Impulse-momentum theorem: net impulse on an object equals its change in momentum. Conservation of momentum: in an isolated system (no net external force), total momentum is conserved: Σpi = Σpf. Collision types: elastic (both momentum and KE conserved), inelastic (momentum conserved, KE not), perfectly inelastic (objects stick together, maximum KE loss). Explosion: reverse of perfectly inelastic collision; total momentum of fragments equals initial momentum (often zero). Two-dimensional collisions: apply conservation of momentum independently in x and y directions. Center of mass: xcm = Σmixi/Σmi; moves as if all mass were concentrated at that point with Fnet = Mtotal × acm. Key concepts: momentum is conserved even when energy is not, identifying the system is crucial for applying conservation laws. Vocabulary: momentum, impulse, conservation of momentum, elastic collision, inelastic collision, perfectly inelastic, recoil, center of mass, isolated system. Common misconceptions: momentum and kinetic energy are the same; heavier objects always have more momentum.

skills.teks_description: Examine simple harmonic motion and wave energy propagation in various media
skills.prompt: This topic covers simple harmonic motion (SHM) for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.8(A)). Students should analyze oscillatory systems and energy in SHM. Definition: SHM occurs when restoring force is proportional to displacement (F = -kx for spring, F = -mg sin(θ) ≈ -mgθ/L for small-angle pendulum). Mass-spring system: T = 2π√(m/k); period depends on mass and spring constant, NOT amplitude. Simple pendulum: T = 2π√(L/g); period depends on length and gravitational field, NOT mass or amplitude (for small angles). Energy in SHM: total energy E = ½kA² is constant; at equilibrium, all KE (maximum velocity); at amplitude, all PE (zero velocity). Velocity at any position: v = ±√(k/m)(A² - x²). Position function: x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ) where ω = 2π/T. Connections between SHM and circular motion: SHM is the projection of uniform circular motion onto one axis. Key concepts: amplitude, period, and frequency are independent in ideal SHM; damping reduces amplitude over time; driven oscillations and resonance (maximum amplitude when driving frequency equals natural frequency). Vocabulary: oscillation, amplitude, period, frequency, restoring force, equilibrium, angular frequency, phase, damping, resonance, natural frequency. Common misconceptions: larger amplitude means longer period; heavier pendulums have different periods.

skills.teks_description: Describe and analyze rotational and circular motion in two dimensions
skills.prompt: This topic covers torque and rotational motion for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.5(D)). Students should analyze rotational dynamics as parallel to translational dynamics. Torque: τ = rF sin(θ) = r⊥F, the rotational equivalent of force; measured in N·m. Sign convention: counterclockwise is typically positive. Rotational kinematics: angular displacement θ, angular velocity ω, angular acceleration α; analogous equations to linear kinematics (θ = ω0t + ½αt², ω = ω0 + αt, ω² = ω0² + 2αθ). Newton's second law for rotation: Στ = Iα, where I is moment of inertia (rotational equivalent of mass). Moment of inertia: I = Σmr² (depends on mass distribution relative to axis); common shapes: point mass I = mr², disk I = ½MR², hoop I = MR², rod about center I = (1/12)ML². Rotational kinetic energy: KErot = ½Iω². Rolling without slipping: v = ωr, total KE = ½mv² + ½Iω². Angular momentum: L = Iω; conserved when no net external torque (ice skater pulling arms in). Static equilibrium: ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0. Vocabulary: torque, moment of inertia, angular velocity, angular acceleration, angular momentum, rotational inertia, lever arm, axis of rotation, rolling, precession. Common misconceptions: torque and force are the same; heavier objects always have more rotational inertia.

skills.teks_description: Compare transverse and longitudinal waves, including electromagnetic and sound waves
skills.prompt: This topic covers mechanical waves and sound for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.8(B)). Students should compare transverse and longitudinal waves and analyze their properties. Mechanical waves require a medium for propagation. Transverse waves: particle displacement perpendicular to wave direction (strings, water surface). Longitudinal waves: particle displacement parallel to wave direction (sound, compression springs). Wave equation: v = fλ. Wave speed depends on medium properties: string v = √(T/μ) where T is tension and μ is linear mass density; sound speed depends on medium density and elasticity (faster in solids than gases). Sound waves: longitudinal pressure waves; speed in air ≈ 343 m/s at 20°C; frequency determines pitch, amplitude determines loudness, waveform determines timbre. Intensity: I = P/A, measured in W/m²; decibel scale is logarithmic. Standing waves on strings: fixed-end boundary conditions create nodes and antinodes; harmonics fn = nv/(2L) for both ends fixed. Standing waves in pipes: open end = antinode, closed end = node. Beat frequency: fbeat = |f1 - f2|. Doppler effect: f' = f(v ± vo)/(v ∓ vs). Vocabulary: transverse, longitudinal, compression, rarefaction, amplitude, frequency, wavelength, standing wave, harmonic, node, antinode, resonance, beats, Doppler effect. Common misconceptions: sound is transverse; louder sounds are higher pitched.

skills.teks_description: Predict electric force magnitude between objects using Coulomb's law
skills.prompt: This topic covers electrostatics for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.6(A)). Students should analyze electric forces and fields using Coulomb's law. Electric charge: fundamental property of matter; positive (protons) and negative (electrons); charge is quantized (q = ne, where e = 1.6 × 10^-19 C) and conserved. Coulomb's law: F = kq1q2/r², where k = 8.99 × 10^9 N·m²/C²; attractive between opposite charges, repulsive between like charges; follows inverse-square law (analogous to gravity). Electric field: E = F/q = kQ/r²; vector quantity pointing in the direction a positive test charge would accelerate. Field lines: originate on positive charges, terminate on negative charges; density indicates field strength; never cross. Superposition: net force or field is the vector sum of individual contributions. Charging methods: friction, conduction, induction. Conductors vs. insulators: in conductors, charges move freely and excess charge resides on surface; in electrostatic equilibrium, E = 0 inside conductor. Key concepts: conservation of charge, Coulomb's law vs. gravitational law comparison, point charge model. Vocabulary: charge, coulomb, electric field, field lines, Coulomb's law, conductor, insulator, grounding, polarization, superposition, electrostatic equilibrium. Common misconceptions: electric field exists only between charges; neutral objects cannot be attracted to charged objects.

skills.teks_description: Analyze and construct series and parallel circuits using schematics
skills.prompt: This topic covers DC circuits for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.6(D)). Students should analyze series and parallel circuits quantitatively. Circuit fundamentals: current I = ΔQ/Δt (charge flow rate, amperes), voltage V (potential difference, volts), resistance R (opposition to current, ohms). Ohm's law: V = IR. Resistors in series: Req = R1 + R2 + ...; same current through all, voltages add. Resistors in parallel: 1/Req = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + ...; same voltage across all, currents add. Kirchhoff's junction rule: ΣI_in = ΣI_out (conservation of charge). Kirchhoff's loop rule: ΣΔV = 0 around any closed loop (conservation of energy). Power: P = IV = I²R = V²/R. Internal resistance: real batteries have internal resistance r; terminal voltage V = emf - Ir. Circuit analysis: draw circuit diagrams, identify series and parallel combinations, apply Kirchhoff's rules for complex circuits. Ammeter: low resistance, connected in series. Voltmeter: high resistance, connected in parallel. Key concepts: brightness of bulbs relates to power dissipated, short circuits, equivalent resistance of combination circuits. Vocabulary: current, voltage, resistance, Ohm's law, series, parallel, equivalent resistance, Kirchhoff's laws, EMF, internal resistance, ammeter, voltmeter, power. Common misconceptions: current is used up in resistors; batteries provide electrons; current flows from negative to positive.

skills.teks_description: Calculate current, potential difference, resistance, and power using Ohm's law
skills.prompt: This topic covers simple circuits and Ohm's law for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.6(E)). Students should calculate current, potential difference, resistance, and power using fundamental circuit relationships. Ohm's law: V = IR relates voltage (potential difference across component), current (through component), and resistance. Ohmic vs. non-ohmic materials: ohmic materials (metals at constant temperature) show linear V-I relationship; non-ohmic (diodes, filament bulbs) do not. Resistance factors: R = ρL/A, where ρ is resistivity (material property), L is length, A is cross-sectional area; resistance increases with temperature for metals. Power in circuits: P = IV = I²R = V²/R; measured in watts. Electrical energy: E = Pt; household energy measured in kilowatt-hours (kWh). Simple circuit analysis: single-loop circuits with battery and resistors; use Ohm's law to find unknown quantities. Key concepts: conventional current direction (positive to negative), electron flow (negative to positive), complete circuit requirement, role of battery as energy source (maintains potential difference), electrical safety (grounding, fuses, circuit breakers). Vocabulary: Ohm's law, resistance, resistivity, current, voltage, power, energy, ohmic, non-ohmic, circuit, load, source, ground. Common misconceptions: larger resistors always dissipate more power; batteries supply constant current.

skills.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy including fluid dynamics and pressure systems
skills.prompt: This topic covers fluid mechanics for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should apply conservation principles to fluid systems. Fluid properties: density ρ = m/V, pressure P = F/A (measured in pascals, Pa). Pressure in fluids: increases with depth, P = P0 + ρgh (hydrostatic pressure); acts equally in all directions at a point (Pascal's principle). Pascal's principle: pressure applied to enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished (hydraulic systems: F1/A1 = F2/A2). Buoyancy: Archimedes' principle states the buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid: Fb = ρfluid × Vdisplaced × g. Object floats when Fb ≥ weight, sinks when Fb < weight. Fluid dynamics: equation of continuity A1v1 = A2v2 (conservation of mass for incompressible fluids); Bernoulli's equation P1 + ½ρv1² + ρgh1 = P2 + ½ρv2² + ρgh2 (conservation of energy for ideal fluids). Bernoulli's principle: where fluid speed increases, pressure decreases (airplane wings, carburetors, shower curtains). Vocabulary: density, pressure, pascal, buoyancy, Archimedes' principle, Pascal's principle, continuity equation, Bernoulli's equation, viscosity, laminar flow, turbulent flow, ideal fluid. Common misconceptions: heavy objects always sink; pressure only pushes down; Bernoulli's principle fully explains airplane lift.

skills.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy using work-energy theorem and thermal transformations
skills.prompt: This topic covers thermodynamics for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should apply conservation of energy principles to thermal systems. Temperature: measure of average translational kinetic energy of molecules; Kelvin scale (K = °C + 273.15). Heat: energy transferred due to temperature difference; flows from hot to cold. Internal energy: total kinetic and potential energy of all molecules in a system. First law of thermodynamics: ΔU = Q - W (change in internal energy = heat added minus work done by system). Work done by gas: W = PΔV (area under P-V curve). Thermodynamic processes: isothermal (constant T, ΔU = 0), isobaric (constant P), isochoric (constant V, W = 0), adiabatic (Q = 0). Heat transfer: conduction, convection, radiation. Specific heat: Q = mcΔT. Latent heat: Q = mL (phase changes at constant temperature). Thermal expansion: materials generally expand when heated. Ideal gas law: PV = nRT connects macroscopic properties. Key concepts: entropy increases in spontaneous processes, heat engines convert thermal energy to work, efficiency is always less than 100%. Vocabulary: temperature, heat, internal energy, entropy, specific heat, latent heat, isothermal, adiabatic, isobaric, isochoric, heat engine, efficiency. Common misconceptions: heat and temperature are the same; cold is a form of energy.

skills.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy including thermal energy transfer processes
skills.prompt: This topic covers heat transfer for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should understand thermal energy transfer processes and perform calculations. Three mechanisms: conduction (energy transfer through direct molecular collisions in solids; rate Q/t = kAΔT/L where k is thermal conductivity), convection (energy transfer by fluid movement; natural convection from density differences, forced convection from external means), radiation (energy transfer via electromagnetic waves; all objects emit thermal radiation; Stefan-Boltzmann law P = εσAT⁴). Thermal equilibrium: objects in contact reach same temperature; zeroth law of thermodynamics. Specific heat capacity: Q = mcΔT; water's high specific heat (4,184 J/kg·K) explains its role as climate moderator. Calorimetry: heat lost by hot object = heat gained by cold object (Qlost = Qgained) in an insulated system. Phase changes: energy absorbed or released without temperature change; Q = mLf (fusion) or Q = mLv (vaporization). Thermal conductors (metals) vs. insulators (wood, foam, air). R-value: measures insulation effectiveness (higher = better insulator). Vocabulary: conduction, convection, radiation, thermal conductivity, specific heat, calorimetry, latent heat, thermal equilibrium, insulator, conductor, emissivity, blackbody. Common misconceptions: metals are inherently cold; insulation adds heat; radiation requires a medium.

skills.teks_description: Calculate and explain work and power; identify when work occurs
skills.prompt: This topic covers work and power for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.7(A)). Students should calculate work and power and identify conditions under which work is performed. Work: W = Fd cos(θ), the product of force and displacement in the direction of force; measured in joules (J). Work is done only when a force causes displacement; no work if force is perpendicular to displacement (e.g., carrying a book horizontally — gravity does no work). Positive work: force component in direction of motion (speeds object up). Negative work: force component opposite to motion (slows object down, e.g., friction). Net work equals change in kinetic energy: Wnet = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mv0². Graphical analysis: work equals area under Force vs. displacement graph (including variable forces like springs). Power: P = W/t = Fv cos(θ), the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred; measured in watts (W). Instantaneous power: P = Fv. Key concepts: distinguish work done by individual forces from net work, work done by variable forces, energy efficiency and power ratings of machines and appliances. Vocabulary: work, joule, power, watt, net work, positive work, negative work, efficiency, force-displacement graph. Common misconceptions: all forces do work; more force always means more work; holding a weight requires work.

skills.teks_description: Analyze momentum conservation in inelastic and elastic collisions
skills.prompt: This topic covers conservation laws for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.7(E)). Students should analyze elastic and inelastic collisions using conservation of momentum and energy. Conservation of momentum: in an isolated system (no net external force), total momentum before interaction equals total momentum after: Σpi = Σpf. Applies to all collisions and explosions. Elastic collisions: both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved; objects bounce apart. For 1D elastic collision between equal masses, objects exchange velocities. Inelastic collisions: momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not (some converts to thermal energy, deformation, sound). Perfectly inelastic: objects stick together; m1v1 + m2v2 = (m1+m2)vf; maximum KE loss. Checking collision type: calculate KE before and after; if equal, elastic; if not, inelastic. Explosions: initially at rest system has total momentum = 0; after explosion, momenta of fragments sum to zero. Two-dimensional collisions: apply conservation of momentum separately in x and y directions. Combined conservation: when both energy and momentum are conserved, you can solve for two unknowns. Vocabulary: elastic collision, inelastic collision, perfectly inelastic, conservation of momentum, conservation of energy, isolated system, recoil, explosion. Common misconceptions: momentum is always conserved (only in isolated systems); elastic collisions are most common (most real collisions are inelastic).

skills.teks_description: Investigate wave characteristics: velocity, frequency, amplitude, wavelength relationships
skills.prompt: This topic covers wave properties for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.8(C)). Students should investigate wave characteristics and the mathematical relationships connecting them. Fundamental wave properties: wavelength λ (distance between consecutive identical points), frequency f (oscillations per second, Hz), amplitude A (maximum displacement from equilibrium), period T = 1/f (time for one complete cycle). Wave equation: v = fλ, wave speed equals frequency times wavelength. Wave speed depends on the medium: string waves v = √(T/μ) where T is tension and μ is linear mass density; speed does not depend on frequency or amplitude. When a wave enters a new medium: speed and wavelength change, frequency stays the same. Energy and amplitude: wave energy is proportional to amplitude squared (E ∝ A²); intensity I = P/A decreases with distance from source (inverse square law for point sources). Wave types: transverse (displacement ⊥ to propagation) and longitudinal (displacement ∥ to propagation). Pulse behavior at boundaries: fixed end reflects inverted, free end reflects upright; partial reflection and transmission at media boundaries. Vocabulary: wavelength, frequency, amplitude, period, wave speed, transverse, longitudinal, pulse, medium, energy, intensity. Common misconceptions: wave speed depends on amplitude or frequency; the medium moves with the wave; higher frequency means higher speed.

skills.teks_description: Investigate wave behaviors including resonance, diffraction, and interference
skills.prompt: This topic covers resonance for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.8(D)). Students should understand resonance, diffraction, and interference phenomena. Resonance: occurs when a driving force has the same frequency as the system's natural frequency, resulting in maximum amplitude of oscillation. Natural frequency: the frequency at which a system oscillates when displaced and released; for mass-spring f = (1/2π)√(k/m), for pendulum f = (1/2π)√(g/L). Resonance examples: pushing a swing at its natural frequency, shattering glass with sound, tuning a radio to a station frequency, Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse. Standing waves as resonance: strings and air columns resonate at specific frequencies (harmonics). Diffraction: bending of waves around obstacles or through openings; more pronounced when opening size is comparable to wavelength. Single-slit diffraction: produces central maximum with decreasing-intensity side maxima. Interference combined with diffraction produces complex patterns. Damping affects resonance: underdamped systems oscillate with decreasing amplitude; critically damped return to equilibrium fastest; overdamped return slowly without oscillation. Quality factor Q describes sharpness of resonance peak. Vocabulary: resonance, natural frequency, driving frequency, damping, standing wave, harmonic, overtone, diffraction, constructive interference, quality factor. Common misconceptions: resonance always causes destruction; diffraction only happens with light; resonance creates energy.

skills.teks_description: Investigate wave behaviors including superposition and interference patterns
skills.prompt: This topic covers the principle of superposition for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.8(D)). Students should understand how waves combine and create interference patterns. Principle of superposition: when two or more waves overlap in the same medium, the resultant displacement at any point is the algebraic sum of the individual displacements. Constructive interference: waves in phase (crests align with crests); amplitudes add; results in larger displacement. Destructive interference: waves out of phase (crests align with troughs); amplitudes subtract; can result in zero displacement (complete cancellation for equal amplitudes). Standing waves: result from superposition of two identical waves traveling in opposite directions; characterized by nodes (zero displacement, destructive interference) and antinodes (maximum displacement, constructive interference). Standing waves on strings: boundary conditions determine allowed wavelengths; harmonics fn = nv/(2L) for fixed-fixed or open-open, fn = nv/(4L) for fixed-open (odd harmonics only). Beats: superposition of two waves with slightly different frequencies produces periodic variation in amplitude; beat frequency = |f1 - f2|. Vocabulary: superposition, constructive interference, destructive interference, standing wave, node, antinode, harmonic, fundamental frequency, overtone, beat frequency, phase. Common misconceptions: waves cancel each other permanently during destructive interference; superposition changes the waves themselves.

skills.teks_description: Investigate wave behaviors: reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, Doppler effect
skills.prompt: This topic covers wave interference for AP Physics 1 students (TEKS Phy.8(D)). Students should understand reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, and the Doppler effect as wave behaviors. Interference patterns: two-source interference produces bright (constructive) and dark (destructive) fringes. Path difference determines type: constructive when path difference = nλ (integer wavelengths), destructive when path difference = (n+½)λ. Double-slit experiment (Young's): d sin(θ) = mλ for maxima, demonstrates wave nature of light. Thin film interference: reflections from top and bottom surfaces interfere; phase shift of π upon reflection from higher-index medium. Reflection: angle of incidence equals angle of reflection; fixed-end reflection inverts wave, free-end does not. Refraction: waves change direction when entering media with different speeds; Snell's law n1 sin(θ1) = n2 sin(θ2). Diffraction: waves spread around obstacles and through openings; more diffraction when opening ≈ wavelength. Doppler effect: observed frequency changes due to relative motion; approaching source = higher frequency, receding = lower frequency; fo = fs(v ± vo)/(v ∓ vs). Polarization: restricting transverse wave oscillation to one plane; only transverse waves can be polarized. Vocabulary: interference, constructive, destructive, path difference, fringe, double-slit, diffraction, refraction, Doppler effect, polarization, thin film. Common misconceptions: interference permanently destroys waves; Doppler effect changes wave speed; refraction always produces a visible bend.
